jokes.text: I got caught up watching this movie in the hotel, "Arabesque," with Sophia Loren and Gregory Peck.
jokes.setup: Larry explains why he missed his flight - should be something urgent or important
jokes.punchline: He was just watching an old movie in his hotel room
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry David selfishness - missing a flight for trivial entertainment. Character-specific and relatable.

jokes.text: You're not golden-ruling it. You're not doing unto others as you would have others do unto you. / Unfortunately, the golden rule is not a federal regulation. / Golden rule trumps everything. Golden rule's on top, federal law is second.
jokes.setup: Normal airline policy about closed doors
jokes.punchline: Larry creates his own hierarchy where the Golden Rule supersedes federal aviation law
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry logic - completely confident in his absurd moral/legal hierarchy. Agent's deadpan response makes it funnier.

jokes.text: A Wetzel's Pretzels. I can't wait to chomp into one of those Wetzel's Pretzels.
jokes.setup: Gate agent tries to cheer him up about airport amenities
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic overenthusiasm for mall pretzels
jokes.explanation: Good delivery of Larry's disdain for fake consolation prizes. The specificity of 'chomp' adds character.

jokes.text: I don't care. That doesn't mean anything. This is filled up. I'm using this. I don't know what to tell you, buddy. It's the Wild West, okay, so.
jokes.setup: Someone objects to Larry taking their overhead bin space
jokes.punchline: Larry declares airplane etiquette is 'the Wild West' to justify his selfishness
jokes.explanation: Larry's ability to rationalize bad behavior with grandiose analogies. 'Wild West' is perfect absurd escalation.

jokes.text: Could you give me, like, a personal upgrade, and I can upgrade you, and... / Sir. / No? Okay.
jokes.setup: Larry tries to bribe flight attendant for first class
jokes.punchline: Flight attendant's stern 'Sir' shuts him down completely
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry pushiness met with institutional authority. The quick deflation is funny but predictable.

jokes.text: I have a medical condition where I have to... my bladder's really overactive. I have a bladder condition, and I have to get up a lot. Is there any way I could sit in the aisle and you would want to switch to the middle? / I can't switch seats with you. I mean, I got the... I can't sit in the middle. It's claustro... claustrophobic in there. / I'll die in the middle.
jokes.setup: Larry claims medical necessity for aisle seat
jokes.punchline: Other passenger also has a condition and will 'die in the middle'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of competing 'medical conditions' - sets up Larry's lie for future consequences.

jokes.text: You are such a gentleman. Thank you so much. / You're welcome. / Heh-hey, my lucky day. / Yeah, mine, too.
jokes.setup: Larry gets what he wants and celebrates
jokes.punchline: Other passenger also thinks it's his lucky day (getting rid of Larry)
jokes.explanation: Perfect mutual satisfaction for opposite reasons. Economy of words with maximum ironic impact.

jokes.text: Hey, they're asking for a doctor. / Mm-mmm. / Huh? You're not gonna heed the call? / Give it a minute. He's gonna be fine. How do you know? You don't even know what he has. You ever been part of an emergency landing? Is that what you want, Larry? To spend the night in Lubbock, Texas, at a Days Inn with a $15 voucher from Cinnabon?
jokes.setup: Medical emergency on plane, doctor refuses to help
jokes.punchline: His elaborate excuse about emergency landings and Cinnabon vouchers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character work - doctor's rationalization is hilariously specific and selfish. 'Cinnabon voucher' detail sells it.

jokes.text: By the way, that's the first time that you got up the entire trip. That was a total bullshit story about the bladder. / I have a medical condition. / Medical. You got a medical condition. She's got a medical condition. One day, you're... you're gonna really have to go to the bathroom, and no one's gonna let you.
jokes.setup: Nathan exposes Larry's fake bladder condition
jokes.punchline: His curse that Larry will be denied bathroom access when he really needs it
jokes.explanation: Good payoff for earlier setup. Nathan's mock repetition and prophetic curse work well together.

jokes.text: Fuck, no. I wouldn't do that shit. You know what, that's not gonna make me wash a car. If anything, I'm not gonna wash it. Just to annoy this guy. I assume it's a guy. A woman wouldn't do something like that. Ladies don't do that kind of shit. Ladies bust windows out and slash tires and key your car and shit.
jokes.setup: Someone wrote 'wash me' on Larry's car
jokes.punchline: Leon's escalating analysis of gender differences in car vandalism
jokes.explanation: Leon's street logic about gendered vandalism is both funny and weirdly insightful. Good character voice.

jokes.text: You like that? See the comma after "no"? / No! / That's good grammar. / You wash me! You wash me! / Yeah. / Yeah. / I like that shit.
jokes.setup: Leon notices grammatical detail in car graffiti
jokes.punchline: He appreciates the proper comma usage in vandalism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Leon moment - finding literary merit in car vandalism. The grammar appreciation is perfectly absurd and character-specific.

jokes.text: Is that one of those "Star Trek" movies? / No, no. It's an old movie with Sophia Loren. She's gorgeous. / Is she naked in the movie? / No, it was... it was from the '60s. Nobody... nobody got naked in the '60s. / She's gotta be naked in something. / Well, if you come across anything, let me know. / You know I'ma look.
jokes.setup: Larry mentions classic movie with beautiful actress
jokes.punchline: Leon's immediate focus on nudity and promise to research it
jokes.explanation: Good character work for Leon's priorities. Larry's '60s observation is historically funny.

jokes.text: You, Manson, Ramirez, and Hitler. That's a good foursome. / If they play fast. / If they play fast. / I'd rather play with a fast Manson than a slow Funkhouser, I'll tell you that. Any day of the week.
jokes.setup: Discussion about playing golf with morally questionable people
jokes.punchline: Larry would rather golf with a mass murderer than slow-playing Funkhouser
jokes.explanation: Peak Larry David moral priority inversion. The casual preference for Hitler over Funkhouser is brilliantly wrong and quotable.

jokes.text: See you off? You're not going to India. You're going to San Francisco! It's an hour away. / So what? / See you off? You'll be back that night.
jokes.setup: Jeff has to take Susie to airport for domestic flight
jokes.punchline: Larry's incredulity at 'seeing off' someone going one hour away for one night
jokes.explanation: Good Larry logic about relationship expectations. The India comparison perfectly highlights the absurdity.

jokes.text: Maybe if you would've seen Cheryl off a few more times, you'd still be together. / I don't think so. / He wants to, trust me. / No, trust me, he doesn't. / Jeff, do you want to? / No. / Well, you're going to.
jokes.setup: Susie blames Larry's divorce on not being attentive enough
jokes.punchline: She overrules Jeff's clear 'no' about wanting to take her to airport
jokes.explanation: Perfect Susie dominance - she asks Jeff what he wants then ignores his answer. Character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: The complexities of who you are? Who are you? You're a Jew from the Bronx!
jokes.setup: Susie claims Larry doesn't understand her complexity
jokes.punchline: Larry reduces her to basic demographics
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry deflation of pretension. Perfect economy - reduces someone to census data in eight words.

jokes.text: You ever called her honey before? / I doubt it. No. / How many dates have you been on? / Two. / Pssh! You just gave a premature honey.
jokes.setup: Marty calls his date 'honey' early in relationship
jokes.punchline: Larry diagnoses it as 'premature honey' like a medical condition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant coinage - 'premature honey' as relationship dysfunction. Original, economical, and becomes episode-driving concept.

jokes.text: Honey's way down the line. You gotta spend a weekend together or something in the country before you can jump to honey. I know what you're trying to do, you're trying to jump ahead with the relationship. But it doesn't work. You got caught.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining proper honey protocol
jokes.punchline: His elaborate rules and accusation of relationship manipulation
jokes.explanation: Larry as relationship referee with arbitrary rules. 'Weekend in the country' is perfect specificity.

jokes.text: Why do I have to clink? / Because it's a custom that people do, which is friendly and nice. / It's a stupid custom. What does it mean, the clinking?
jokes.setup: Group wants to toast, Larry refuses to clink glasses
jokes.punchline: His demand for explanation of clinking's meaning
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry social contrarianism. Questioning basic social rituals is on-brand but predictable.

jokes.text: You know, you pick up a knife like this, a steak knife, you get the urge... I do... that I wanna... I wanna stab... I wanna stab with it. Yeah, 'cause you pick up a baseball bat, you wanna swing it. You pick up a tennis ball, you wanna throw it. When you have a... a knife, you wanna stab, do you not?
jokes.setup: Larry picks up a knife at dinner
jokes.punchline: His theory about objects creating urges to use them violently
jokes.explanation: Dark but relatable observation delivered with Larry's casual tone. The comparison to sports equipment normalizes the violent urge.

jokes.text: Know what they should have? What's that? A stabbing range. Get out of here. Yeah, a stabbing range where you can stab... stab dummies. You'd feel good. You'd feel good. Where would the stabbing range be? In the Valley.
jokes.setup: Larry's knife urge theory
jokes.punchline: His business proposal for a stabbing range in the Valley
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from dark thought to business plan. 'In the Valley' adds perfect LA specificity to absurd concept.

jokes.text: No filter. / Huh, interesting. Surprised you don't have a filter. You have no filter. / Huh, that's... yeah. / Isn't it obvious? That's true. You just say whatever comes into your mind. You don't really modulate your inner feelings at all, do you? It's a good thing for me, but it's a bad thing for water.
jokes.setup: Marilyn serves unfiltered tap water
jokes.punchline: Larry connects it to her personality - good for him, bad for water
jokes.explanation: Excellent dual meaning of 'filter' - water filtration and social filtering. The final line perfectly caps the observation.

jokes.text: Goldfish would commit suicide in this water.
jokes.setup: Larry tastes terrible tap water
jokes.punchline: Even goldfish would kill themselves rather than live in it
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - maximum impact in seven words. The absurd image of suicidal goldfish is memorable and dark.

jokes.text: You're like Fred MacMurray in "The Caine Mutiny" when he and Van Johnson go to see the admiral, then Fred MacMurray gets cold feet and he backs out. / That's a movie, this is real life. In real life, you're polite to your hostess.
jokes.setup: Marty compares Larry to movie coward
jokes.punchline: Larry distinguishes between movie heroism and real-world politeness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's twisted logic about social propriety vs. honesty.

jokes.text: I want you to go. / I'm getting kicked out? / Yeah, I think... / You're kicking me out? / Yeah, I think you should leave. / This is unbelievable. Okay. Well, it was fun. We... we really... / It was not fun. / No, it wasn't. I leave my knife and I bid you adieu.
jokes.setup: Dinner party reaches breaking point
jokes.punchline: Larry's formal farewell with knife reference after being kicked out
jokes.explanation: Good escalation to social disaster. The formal 'bid you adieu' with knife callback shows Larry's priorities.

jokes.text: Hey, bald fuck! / Bald fuck! / Blow me, blow me! / You wash it! You wash it! / Blow me, bald fuck!
jokes.setup: Kids see Larry and his dirty car
jokes.punchline: They turn car graffiti into coordinated insults
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation of car vandalism theme. Kids being crude isn't particularly clever but serves plot.

jokes.text: You saw... you saw a wife there and you seized on it with a honey, right? That's what you did. / It was a seize. / You seized. / I did seize. / You did seize.
jokes.setup: Larry analyzing Marty's honey mistake
jokes.punchline: They both embrace 'seize' as the perfect verb for premature relationship escalation
jokes.explanation: Good callback with word play. The mutual agreement on 'seize' as diagnostic term works well.

jokes.text: What ever comes good after "we need to talk"? Nothing. You ever hear this? "We need to talk. I'm taking you to Cancun." No, that's not happening. Is that gonna happen? No. No. That's not gonna happen.
jokes.setup: Rhonda texts 'we need to talk' to Marty
jokes.punchline: Larry's imaginary positive follow-up to that phrase
jokes.explanation: Good universal relationship observation. The Cancun example is absurd enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: When she comes in, you start calling everybody honey. I do what? Call everybody honey... the waitresses, the manager, customers, everyone. Why? Because... 'cause you're a guy who just uses the word honey a lot. You mean it's part of my working vocabulary? Yes! Yes!
jokes.setup: Larry proposes solution to honey problem
jokes.punchline: Make honey part of Marty's 'working vocabulary' so it means nothing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry scheme - dilute the honey's meaning through overuse. 'Working vocabulary' is perfect clinical term.

jokes.text: That means that she's just part of the honey parade.
jokes.setup: Explaining the honey strategy
jokes.punchline: 'Honey parade' as metaphor for meaningless endearments
jokes.explanation: Perfect phrase - 'honey parade' captures the meaninglessness beautifully. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: Honey, do you mind if I grab a chair? Thank you, honey. / Oh, no, I don't... um, thanks. / Excuse me, honey. Can I get... / A black coffee, honey. / Oh, honey, right here. / Oh, thank you so much. Honey, can you bring a turkey burger and some fries? / Thanks, honey. Hey, hon! / You know, honey, can we get an extra napkin? / Hey, could I get some coffee, honey? / Honey? Honey? / Thank you, honey. / Could we get the check, honey? / Thank you, honey.
jokes.setup: Marty implements Larry's honey strategy
jokes.punchline: Absurd overuse of 'honey' with everyone in restaurant
jokes.explanation: Great execution of the honey strategy. The repetition creates mounting absurdity as plan works too well.

jokes.text: Accidental text on purpose. / Please explain. / You're sending a text to me, but it goes to her "by accident." And in the text, you say, "Hey, LD, I'd love to go to the game, but I'm taking Susie to the airport. I don't wanna disappoint her. We're getting along so great."
jokes.setup: Jeff has problem with Susie's airport demand
jokes.punchline: Larry invents 'accidental text on purpose' scheme
jokes.explanation: Episode title concept - brilliant manipulation scheme. The phrase 'accidental text on purpose' is perfectly contradictory.

jokes.text: You, sir, are an amazing man. I am texting Susie right now.
jokes.setup: Larry explains his scheme
jokes.punchline: Jeff immediately implements it with admiration
jokes.explanation: Jeff's immediate adoption shows how appealing Larry's schemes are to desperate husbands.

jokes.text: You send me a text, but you accidentally send it to her on purpose. And in the text, you say something like, "Hey, Larry, you were an asshole the other night. There's nothing wrong with her water. It's perfectly okay."
jokes.setup: Larry explains accidental text concept to Marty
jokes.punchline: Using it to fix the water filter disaster
jokes.explanation: Good application of the scheme to earlier problem. Shows Larry's systems thinking about social manipulation.

jokes.text: I invented this. I invented this.
jokes.setup: Multiple people want to use accidental text strategy
jokes.punchline: Larry claims credit for inventing deceptive communication
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry pride in dubious achievements. Taking credit for manipulation scheme is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: She texted me right back. "I love you. I know this is not meant for me. What a great man to stick up for me like that."
jokes.setup: Accidental text scheme in action
jokes.punchline: Marilyn falls for it completely and declares love
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff showing scheme works too well. Her response is believably overwrought for someone 'accidentally' reading defense.

jokes.text: Oh, so when you're losing money, you heed the call, is that it? You know what that is? Selective heeding, that's what that is!
jokes.setup: Nathan leaves golf game claiming medical emergency
jokes.punchline: Larry calls out his hypocrisy as 'selective heeding'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with perfect phrase - 'selective heeding' captures the hypocrisy perfectly. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: It's just perfume. ... out of nowhere, right in my face, spritzing me. They're the worst. Come on, what is that? What a violation. You know what, they'll usually go away, though, if you just wave them away. Did you wave her away? Sort of, yeah.
jokes.setup: Larry explains perfume lady encounter
jokes.punchline: His understated 'sort of' for what was apparently pushing
jokes.explanation: Sets up the spritzer incident that will cause problems. Larry's minimization of his aggression is character-typical.

jokes.text: Of course I did. What do you mean of course you did? I got attacked and I pushed. You don't push a spritzer lady! You're out of your fucking mind! It's a violation! You don't just go up to people and spritz them in the face like that.
jokes.setup: Marty learns Larry pushed perfume lady
jokes.punchline: Larry's complete confidence in his justified retaliation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry self-righteousness about antisocial behavior. His 'violation' rhetoric treats perfume like assault.

jokes.text: It's either Larry David or Marilyn. She gave you an ultimatum? An ultimatum. Who'd you pick? I took Marilyn. I would've made the same decision. Let's face it, at this point in our lives, it's... it's not so easy to find people to have sex with us.
jokes.setup: Marty chose girlfriend over Larry friendship
jokes.punchline: Larry's brutally honest acceptance based on sexual prospects
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny resignation to aging and sexual reality. Larry's brutal honesty about middle-aged dating market.

jokes.text: Hey, vagina always wins. I lost a brother to a vagina.
jokes.setup: Discussion of choosing girlfriends over male friends
jokes.punchline: Leon's crude but philosophical take on relationship priorities
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leon wisdom - crude but accurate. 'Lost a brother to a vagina' is memorably economical phrasing.

jokes.text: When women give me an ultimatum, "Who are you gonna choose, me or your friend?" I say, "I'm gonna choose my friend." That's it. / Why do you have to do this? You dumped me. You chose somebody over me.
jokes.setup: Larry claims he'd choose friends over women
jokes.punchline: Marty points out Larry is currently single because he does exactly this
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - Larry's principles explain his romantic failures. Marty's response deflates his righteousness.

jokes.text: Oh! Oh, I'm... no! Oh! No! That's not from you. That... that's from Sophia Loren. / Why was it in my face? / Because it was an accident! It was an accidental erection.
jokes.setup: Larry gets erection while trying on pants with Rhonda present
jokes.punchline: He blames Sophia Loren and calls it 'accidental erection'
jokes.explanation: Great payoff for the Sophia Loren setup. 'Accidental erection' parallels the episode's accidental text theme.

jokes.text: No, an accident is tripping on a step, and then also the Titanic going down. / Yeah, but just the way the Titanic went down, this thing went up. It's the same... it's the same thing. They're both accidents. / So, you're saying your penis is like the Titanic? / In a manner of speaking, yes. I think they're... they're comparable.
jokes.setup: Debate over whether erections can be accidental
jokes.punchline: Larry compares his penis to the Titanic disaster
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. Comparing an erection to maritime disaster is peak Larry logic. Perfectly quotable.

jokes.text: You know, I gotta say, the waist is a little big. / Get out. / I'll wear them with a belt. I'll put a belt on.
jokes.setup: Larry being thrown out after erection incident
jokes.punchline: He still tries to negotiate the pants fit while being ejected
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry priorities - still focused on consumer satisfaction while being kicked out for sexual harassment.

jokes.text: Ignore him? That's a 14-foot-long cock.
jokes.setup: Susie tells Jeff to ignore Larry at dinner
jokes.punchline: Larry reveals someone drew giant penis on his car
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from 'wash me' to giant penis. Larry's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Oh, dear. Premature girlfriend. Oh, no, I know... no, no, no. Listen, Richard, you know what, I think we're just looking for other things right now. / I call everybody girlfriend. It's a thing of mine. Hi, girlfriend. Girlfriend, hi. / Honey! Honey, come on.
jokes.setup: Richard calls Rhonda 'girlfriend' too early
jokes.punchline: He tries Larry's defense strategies but she's having none of it
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing the terminology scheme doesn't always work. Richard's desperation is believable.

jokes.text: What... what is an accidental text on purpose? / You send somebody a text, pretending it to be to somebody else so they think they're reading a private text. But, really, what they're trying to do is give you the information that they want to manipulate you with. It's bullshit.
jokes.setup: Susie exposes the accidental text scheme
jokes.punchline: Her complete understanding and dismissal of the manipulation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation where the scheme gets exposed. Susie's clinical analysis deflates Larry's 'invention.'

jokes.text: I can't stand your water. I can't... I don't even know what to say. It's like I took a straw and put it in a frog's ass. It makes me sick! I wanna barf every time I get near it. I can't stand the smell, I can't stand the color, and I cannot stand the taste. I can't take it anymore!
jokes.setup: Marilyn demands Larry's honest opinion about her water
jokes.punchline: His explosive, detailed disgust with specific frog's ass comparison
jokes.explanation: Explosive release of Larry's true feelings. 'Frog's ass' comparison is memorably disgusting and specific.

jokes.text: This is a comedy emergency. I'm off duty. / Why won't you help me? / You didn't heed the call on the plane. You welched on a $200 bet. You sent me an accidental text on purpose. I invented the accidental text, okay?
jokes.setup: Nathan wants Larry's help with his speech
jokes.punchline: Larry lists all of Nathan's betrayals, claiming invention of accidental text
jokes.explanation: Good convergence of storylines. Larry's catalog of grievances and invention claim works well.

jokes.text: You're a psychopath who uses a weak bladder to take advantage of people.
jokes.setup: Larry exposes Nathan's fake bladder condition
jokes.punchline: His clinical diagnosis of Nathan as manipulative psychopath
jokes.explanation: Good final exposure of Nathan's hypocrisy. 'Psychopath' escalation fits Larry's dramatic style.

jokes.text: I look at a dirty car, Larry, and I feel sorry for it. It's almost like it's screaming out, "Wash me! Wash me." / Good to see you, Larry.
jokes.setup: Neighbor Dave seems sympathetic about car wash situation
jokes.punchline: He reveals he's the one writing messages on Larry's car
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - the sympathetic neighbor is the culprit. His earnest car empathy makes the reveal funnier.
